## 🔧 Storybook & Compodoc Integration with Angular

### 📌 Overview

Storybook for Angular is a framework that makes it easy to develop and test UI components in isolation. It uses Angular builders and integrates with Compodoc to provide automatic documentation generation.

To make the most out of Storybook in your Angular project, you can set up Compodoc integration and Storybook decorators based on your project needs.

---

### 🚀 Installation

When installing Storybook via `npx storybook@latest init`, you can set up Compodoc **automatically**. If you have already installed Storybook, you can set up Compodoc **manually**.

---

### ⚙️ Configuring `angular.json` for Compodoc

To enable Compodoc manually, update your `angular.json` with the `compodoc` flag and `compodocArgs`:

Add `"compodoc": true` and `"compodocArgs"` to the builder options. The `-d` path is where the generated documentation is stored — usually the root of your Angular project (not necessarily the root of your Angular Workspace).

For performance optimization, you can extend `compodocArgs` with additional flags:

In Angular, Storybook uses Compodoc to generate the documentation displayed in the stories and controls. Sometimes the generation becomes slow, especially if you are constantly re-doing or improving the documentation.

You can speed it up by adding flags such as `--minimal`, `--disablePrivate`, `--disableInternal`, `--disableSourceCode`, and `--disableLifeCycleHooks` to your `compodocArgs`.

---

### 🔄 Updating `package.json` Scripts

Note that Compodoc is now **built into `@storybook/angular`**; you don't have to call it explicitly.

If you were running Compodoc in your `package.json` scripts, you can remove the related script (e.g., `"docs:json": "compodoc -p tsconfig.json -e json -d ./documentation"`) and replace your storybook scripts with `ng run <project-name>:storybook`.

---

### 📝 Using JSDoc for Documentation

You can include JSDoc comments above components, directives, and other parts of your Angular code to include documentation for those elements. Compodoc uses these comments to generate documentation for your application.

In Storybook, it is useful to add explanatory comments above `@Inputs` and `@Outputs`, since these are the main elements that Storybook displays in its user interface — such as controls.

---

### 🗂️ Configuring `preview.js`/`preview.ts`

Go to your `.storybook/preview.js` and add the following to wire up Compodoc JSON:
```js
import { setCompodocJson } from '@storybook/addon-docs/angular';
import docJson from '../documentation.json';
setCompodocJson(docJson);
```

---

### 🏗️ Multi-Project Workspaces

In case you have multiple projects, you will have to adjust your `angular.json` and `package.json` for each project you want to use Storybook with. Each project should have a dedicated `.storybook` folder placed at the project's root directory. You can run `npx storybook@latest init` sequentially for each project.

You can then combine multiple Storybooks with **Storybook Composition**.

---

### 🧩 Nx Workspace Support

There is also a dedicated guide for setting up Compodoc for Storybook in Angular projects within an **Nx workspace**.

Nx also provides a **Storybook 10 migration generator** to help upgrade existing setups.

You can generate Storybook configuration for an individual Angular project using the `@nx/angular:storybook-configuration` generator. It has the option to automatically generate `*.stories.ts` files for each component declared in the library, using **Component Story Format 3 (CSF3)**.

---

### ⚠️ Known Issues

A known bug exists where Storybook cannot find the Compodoc documentation for components within an Angular **Library** project setup, causing it to fail to generate the correct args and descriptions.

---

### 🎯 Key Benefits of the Integration

The integration offers: **Automatic Documentation** from TypeScript code, **Better Controls** based on `@Input` properties, **Type Safety** via TypeScript types, **JSDoc Integration** for rich descriptions, and **Less Maintenance** since documentation stays in sync with your code.

## 🧩 Nx Storybook Angular + Compodoc Setup & Storybook 10 Configuration

---

### 1. 📦 Generating Storybook Configuration for an Angular Project

You can generate Storybook configuration for an individual Angular project by using the `@nx/angular:storybook-configuration` generator.

This generator will set up Storybook for your Angular project. By default, **Storybook v10** is used.

Make sure to use the framework-specific generators if your project is using Angular, React, Next.js, Vue, Nuxt, or React Native: `@nx/angular:storybook-configuration`, `@nx/react:storybook-configuration`, `@nx/vue:storybook-configuration`.

---

### 2. 📁 What the Generator Creates

Nx will generate a project-level `.storybook` folder (located under `libs/your-project/.storybook` or `apps/your-project/.storybook`) containing the essential configuration files for Storybook.

Nx will create new targets in your project's `project.json`, called `storybook`, `test-storybook`, and `build-storybook`, containing all the necessary configuration to serve, test, and build Storybook.

The `@nx/angular:storybook-configuration` generator has the option to automatically generate `*.stories.ts` files for each component declared in the library. The stories will be generated using **Component Story Format 3 (CSF3)**.

---

### 3. 🔧 Angular `browserTarget` Configuration

You will notice that `browserTarget` is specified for the `storybook` and `build-storybook` targets. Angular needs the `browserTarget` for Storybook in order to know which configuration to use for the build.

If your project is buildable (it has a build target, and uses the main Angular builder — `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser`, `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application`, or `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser-esbuild`) the `browserTarget` for Storybook will use the build target; if it's not buildable (or is using another Angular builder) it will use the `build-storybook` target.

You do not have to do anything manually. Nx will create the configuration for you.

---

### 4. 📖 Setting Up Compodoc with Storybook

The Nx guide explains how to set up Compodoc for Storybook in Angular projects within your Nx workspace.

Compodoc is a documentation tool for Angular applications. When used with Storybook, Compodoc can automatically generate documentation for your Angular components by analyzing their TypeScript code, decorators, and JSDoc comments.

**Key benefits of Compodoc + Storybook:**

- **Automatic Documentation**: Compodoc generates component documentation automatically from your TypeScript code.
- **Better Controls**: Enhanced Storybook controls based on your component's `@Input` properties.
- **Type Safety**: Leverages TypeScript types for accurate documentation.
- **JSDoc Integration**: Uses JSDoc comments for rich descriptions.
- **Less Maintenance**: Documentation stays in sync with your code.


---

### 5. 🛠️ Manual Compodoc Configuration (in `angular.json` / `project.json`)

If you have already installed Storybook, you can set up Compodoc manually.

You need to set `"compodoc": true` and provide `compodocArgs` in both the `storybook` and `build-storybook` builder options. Storybook for Angular uses Angular builders and integrates with Compodoc to provide automatic documentation generation.

To speed up documentation generation, you can also add optional flags. In Angular, Storybook uses Compodoc to generate the documentation displayed in the stories and controls. Sometimes the generation becomes slow, especially if you are constantly re-doing the documentation. You can mitigate this with flags like `--minimal`, `--disablePrivate`, `--disableInternal`, `--disableSourceCode`, and `--disableLifeCycleHooks` in `compodocArgs`.

---

### 6. 📝 tsconfig for Compodoc

When using Compodoc, you need to create a `tsconfig` file, and in the `include` array you need to place all the files that you want Compodoc to include in its compilation. Compodoc suggests adding a `tsconfig.doc.json` for this purpose. Then, when running Compodoc, you can use the `-p` (or `--tsconfig`) flag to specify the path to that file.

---

### 7. 📄 JSDoc Comments for Rich Documentation

In Storybook, it makes sense to add explanatory comments above your `@Inputs` and `@Outputs`, since these are the main elements that Storybook focuses on — the controls.

When you run Compodoc, it will generate a `documentation.json` file. This file is then consumed by Storybook to display rich docs.

---

### 8. ⚙️ `preview.ts` Integration

After generating the `documentation.json`, you need to configure your `.storybook/preview.ts` to load it:
In your `.storybook/preview.ts`, import `setCompodocJson` from `@storybook/addon-docs/angular` and import the generated `documentation.json`, then call `setCompodocJson(docJson)`.

---

### 9. 🔄 Storybook 10 Migration

For Storybook-specific migrations that are not automatically handled by Nx, refer to the official Storybook page's **Upgrading Storybook guide** and the **Storybook 10 migration generator**.

Even if you are migrating from an older version of Nx, Nx will make sure to change your `package.json` Storybook targets to match the new schema.

---

Here is a comprehensive summary of the search results on **Nx + Storybook + Angular + Compodoc setup and Storybook 10 configuration**:

---

## 🧩 Nx Storybook Angular + Compodoc Setup & Storybook 10 Configuration

---

### 1. 📦 Generating Storybook Configuration for an Angular Project

You can generate Storybook configuration for an individual Angular project by using the `@nx/angular:storybook-configuration` generator.

This generator will set up Storybook for your Angular project. By default, **Storybook v10** is used.

Make sure to use the framework-specific generators if your project is using Angular, React, Next.js, Vue, Nuxt, or React Native: `@nx/angular:storybook-configuration`, `@nx/react:storybook-configuration`, `@nx/vue:storybook-configuration`.

---

### 2. 📁 What the Generator Creates

Nx will generate a project-level `.storybook` folder (located under `libs/your-project/.storybook` or `apps/your-project/.storybook`) containing the essential configuration files for Storybook.

Nx will create new targets in your project's `project.json`, called `storybook`, `test-storybook`, and `build-storybook`, containing all the necessary configuration to serve, test, and build Storybook.

The `@nx/angular:storybook-configuration` generator has the option to automatically generate `*.stories.ts` files for each component declared in the library. The stories will be generated using **Component Story Format 3 (CSF3)**.

---

### 3. 🔧 Angular `browserTarget` Configuration

You will notice that `browserTarget` is specified for the `storybook` and `build-storybook` targets. Angular needs the `browserTarget` for Storybook in order to know which configuration to use for the build.

If your project is buildable (it has a build target, and uses the main Angular builder — `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser`, `@angular-devkit/build-angular:application`, or `@angular-devkit/build-angular:browser-esbuild`) the `browserTarget` for Storybook will use the build target; if it's not buildable (or is using another Angular builder) it will use the `build-storybook` target.

You do not have to do anything manually. Nx will create the configuration for you.

---

### 4. 📖 Setting Up Compodoc with Storybook

The Nx guide explains how to set up Compodoc for Storybook in Angular projects within your Nx workspace.

Compodoc is a documentation tool for Angular applications. When used with Storybook, Compodoc can automatically generate documentation for your Angular components by analyzing their TypeScript code, decorators, and JSDoc comments.

**Key benefits of Compodoc + Storybook:**

- **Automatic Documentation**: Compodoc generates component documentation automatically from your TypeScript code.
- **Better Controls**: Enhanced Storybook controls based on your component's `@Input` properties.
- **Type Safety**: Leverages TypeScript types for accurate documentation.
- **JSDoc Integration**: Uses JSDoc comments for rich descriptions.
- **Less Maintenance**: Documentation stays in sync with your code.


---

### 5. 🛠️ Manual Compodoc Configuration (in `angular.json` / `project.json`)

If you have already installed Storybook, you can set up Compodoc manually.

You need to set `"compodoc": true` and provide `compodocArgs` in both the `storybook` and `build-storybook` builder options. Storybook for Angular uses Angular builders and integrates with Compodoc to provide automatic documentation generation.

To speed up documentation generation, you can also add optional flags. In Angular, Storybook uses Compodoc to generate the documentation displayed in the stories and controls. Sometimes the generation becomes slow, especially if you are constantly re-doing the documentation. You can mitigate this with flags like `--minimal`, `--disablePrivate`, `--disableInternal`, `--disableSourceCode`, and `--disableLifeCycleHooks` in `compodocArgs`.

---

### 6. 📝 tsconfig for Compodoc

When using Compodoc, you need to create a `tsconfig` file, and in the `include` array you need to place all the files that you want Compodoc to include in its compilation. Compodoc suggests adding a `tsconfig.doc.json` for this purpose. Then, when running Compodoc, you can use the `-p` (or `--tsconfig`) flag to specify the path to that file.

---

### 7. 📄 JSDoc Comments for Rich Documentation

In Storybook, it makes sense to add explanatory comments above your `@Inputs` and `@Outputs`, since these are the main elements that Storybook focuses on — the controls.

When you run Compodoc, it will generate a `documentation.json` file. This file is then consumed by Storybook to display rich docs.

---

### 8. ⚙️ `preview.ts` Integration

After generating the `documentation.json`, you need to configure your `.storybook/preview.ts` to load it:
In your `.storybook/preview.ts`, import `setCompodocJson` from `@storybook/addon-docs/angular` and import the generated `documentation.json`, then call `setCompodocJson(docJson)`.

---

### 9. 🔄 Storybook 10 Migration

For Storybook-specific migrations that are not automatically handled by Nx, refer to the official Storybook page's **Upgrading Storybook guide** and the **Storybook 10 migration generator**.

Even if you are migrating from an older version of Nx, Nx will make sure to change your `package.json` Storybook targets to match the new schema.

# Setting Up Compodoc with Storybook in Nx Angular

Based on the provided documentation, here's a concise guide to the setup process:

## Overview

"Compodoc is a documentation tool for Angular applications" that "automatically generates documentation by analyzing TypeScript interfaces and classes, reading Angular decorators, and parsing JSDoc comments."

## Installation Steps

1. **Install the package**: Run `npm install --save-dev @compodoc/compodoc` to add Compodoc as a development dependency.

2. **Configure Storybook's main.ts**: Update `.storybook/main.ts` with TypeScript configuration settings including `reactDocgen: 'react-docgen-typescript'` and enable autodocs.

3. **Add npm scripts**: Modify `package.json` to include a docs generation command (`compodoc -p tsconfig.json -e json -d .`) that runs before Storybook starts.

## Component Setup

Document your Angular components using:
- JSDoc comments above the `@Component` decorator
- TypeScript interfaces defining component properties
- JSDoc annotations on each `@Input` and `@Output` property

## Story Configuration

Create story files that define `argTypes` with appropriate controls (select, text, etc.) and provide multiple story variants demonstrating different component states and configurations.

## Key Benefits

The integration provides "automatic documentation generation, enhanced Storybook controls based on component inputs, type safety through TypeScript, and reduced maintenance overhead by keeping documentation synchronized with source code."

# Angular Compodoc Setup for Storybook on Nx - Concise Guide

## Overview
"Compodoc is a documentation tool for Angular applications" that "automatically generates documentation by analyzing TypeScript interfaces and classes" and "reading Angular decorators."

## Key Installation Steps

1. **Install Compodoc**: Add it as a development dependency via npm
2. **Configure Storybook's main.ts**: Update `.storybook/main.ts` with TypeScript and documentation settings
3. **Add npm scripts**: Create a docs generation command that runs before Storybook starts
4. **Document components**: Use JSDoc comments and TypeScript interfaces on Angular components

## Essential Configuration Elements

The guide covers:
- Storybook framework configuration for Angular projects
- TypeScript compiler options and docgen settings
- Autodocs enablement in the docs section
- Component decorator analysis (`@Input`, `@Output`)
- JSDoc comment parsing for rich descriptions

## Component Documentation Pattern

Components should leverage TypeScript interfaces, JSDoc comments on properties, and Angular decorators to enable automatic documentation generation. The example demonstrates a button component with multiple input properties and event outputs.

## Story Configuration

Story files integrate generated type information into Storybook's `argTypes`, enabling interactive controls that reflect component properties and their types.

## Primary Benefits

The approach provides automatic documentation generation, improved Storybook controls, type safety, JSDoc integration, and reduced maintenance burden through code-synchronized documentation.

# Plan: Set Up Compodoc with Storybook in Nx Angular Library ## Strategic Direction 1. Add `@compodoc/compodoc@^1.2.1` to `devDependencies` in `package.json`; transitive deps are not version-pinned and may vanish on lockfile changes. 2. Create `packages/ngx-smart-components/tsconfig.doc.json` extending `tsconfig.lib.json` with `"include": ["src/**/*.ts"]` and `"exclude": ["**/*.spec.ts", "**/*.stories.ts"]`. 3. Set `compodoc: true` and `compodocArgs: ["-e", "json", "-d", "packages/ngx-smart-components", "-p", "packages/ngx-smart-components/tsconfig.doc.json"]` on both `storybook` and `build-storybook` targets; Nx executes from workspace root. 4. Add `'@storybook/addon-docs'` to `addons` in `.storybook/main.ts`; Storybook 10 requires explicit registration. 5. Populate `.storybook/preview.ts` with `import { setCompodocJson } from '@storybook/addon-docs/angular'; import docJson from '../documentation.json'; setCompodocJson(docJson); export const parameters = { docs: { source: { type: 'code' } } };`. 6. Add `"../documentation.json"` to `include` in `.storybook/tsconfig.json` and enable `"resolveJsonModule": true`. 7. Replace component with `readonly label = input('default'); readonly clicked = output();` plus JSDoc `/** ... */` blocks above class, each input, and each output. 8. Add `tags: ['autodocs']` to the story default export; Compodoc supplies descriptions, autodocs renders Docs tab. 9. Run `npm install && npx nx run ngx-smart-components:storybook`. ## Steps 1. **Add @compodoc/compodoc as a direct devDependency** - File: `package.json` - Change: Add `"@compodoc/compodoc": "^1.2.1"` to `devDependencies`. Currently installed as a transitive dep at 1.2.1; pinning it directly prevents version drift on lockfile regeneration. - Rationale: Advisor step 1 -- transitive deps are not version-pinned and can silently change. 2. **Create tsconfig.doc.json for Compodoc** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/tsconfig.doc.json` - Change: Create a new file extending `tsconfig.lib.json` that includes `src/**/*.ts` and excludes spec and story files. Compodoc uses this tsconfig to find and parse Angular source. - Content: ```json { "extends": "./tsconfig.lib.json", "include": ["src/**/*.ts"], "exclude": ["**/*.spec.ts", "**/*.stories.ts"] } ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 2 -- Compodoc needs an explicit tsconfig scoped to source-only files. 3. **Enable Compodoc in project.json storybook targets** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/project.json` - Change: On both `storybook` and `build-storybook` targets, change `"compodoc": false` to `"compodoc": true` and add `"compodocArgs"`. Because Nx runs from the workspace root, all paths in `compodocArgs` must be workspace-relative: ```json "compodoc": true, "compodocArgs": [ "-e", "json", "-d", "packages/ngx-smart-components", "-p", "packages/ngx-smart-components/tsconfig.doc.json" ] ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 3 -- `-d` sets where `documentation.json` is written (the project root); `-p` points Compodoc at the correct tsconfig. 4. **Register @storybook/addon-docs in main.ts** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/.storybook/main.ts` - Change: Add `'@storybook/addon-docs'` to the `addons` array. The current array is empty. - Result: ```ts addons: ['@storybook/addon-docs'], ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 4 -- Storybook 10 requires explicit addon registration; without this the Docs tab does not appear. 5. **Wire setCompodocJson in preview.ts** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/.storybook/preview.ts` - Change: Replace the empty file with: ```ts import type { Preview } from '@storybook/angular'; import { setCompodocJson } from '@storybook/addon-docs/angular'; import docJson from '../documentation.json'; setCompodocJson(docJson); const preview: Preview = { parameters: { docs: { source: { type: 'code' }, }, }, }; export default preview; ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 5 -- `setCompodocJson` loads the Compodoc output into Storybook so JSDoc descriptions appear in the Docs tab. 6. **Update .storybook/tsconfig.json to resolve documentation.json** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/.storybook/tsconfig.json` - Change: Add `"resolveJsonModule": true` to `compilerOptions` and add `"../documentation.json"` to the `include` array so TypeScript can import the generated JSON file. - Rationale: Advisor step 6 -- without `resolveJsonModule` the `import docJson from '../documentation.json'` in preview.ts will fail to compile. 7. **Add signal inputs/outputs and JSDoc to the component** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/src/lib/ngx-smart-components/ngx-smart-components.ts` - Change: Import `input` and `output` from `@angular/core`, add JSDoc above the class and above each signal property: ```ts import { ChangeDetectionStrategy, Component, ViewEncapsulation, input, output, } from '@angular/core'; /** * A smart component that demonstrates Compodoc integration with Storybook. */ @Component({ selector: 'smart-ngx-smart-components', imports: [], template: `

{{ label() }}

`, styles: ` :host { display: block; } `, encapsulation: ViewEncapsulation.Emulated, changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush, }) export class NgxSmartComponents { /** The label text displayed inside the component. */ readonly label = input('NgxSmartComponents works!'); /** Emitted when the component is clicked. */ readonly clicked = output(); } ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 7 -- signal-based `input()` and `output()` are the modern Angular API; JSDoc above class and each signal is what Compodoc reads to populate the Docs tab descriptions. 8. **Add autodocs tag to the story** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/src/lib/ngx-smart-components/ngx-smart-components.stories.ts` - Change: Add `tags: ['autodocs']` to the `meta` object so Storybook generates the Docs page. Also update the `Primary` story's `args` to exercise the `label` input: ```ts const meta: Meta = { component: NgxSmartComponents, title: 'NgxSmartComponents', tags: ['autodocs'], }; ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 8 -- `tags: ['autodocs']` triggers Storybook's autodocs renderer; Compodoc provides the JSDoc descriptions that populate the auto-generated Docs page. 9. **Install dependencies and verify** - Command: `npm install && npx nx run ngx-smart-components:storybook` - Rationale: Advisor step 9 -- `npm install` pins `@compodoc/compodoc` as a direct dep; `nx run storybook` triggers Compodoc to generate `documentation.json` then serves Storybook with the Docs tab populated. ## Key Decisions - **`@storybook/addon-docs` explicit registration**: Storybook 10 no longer auto-includes addon-docs; it must be listed in `addons`. Skipping this causes a blank/missing Docs tab. - **`-d packages/ngx-smart-components` for `compodocArgs`**: Compodoc writes `documentation.json` to the directory specified by `-d`. The path must be workspace-root-relative because Nx executes the executor from the workspace root. Setting it to the project root means `preview.ts` can import it as `../documentation.json`. - **`tsconfig.doc.json` as a separate tsconfig**: Using the library tsconfig directly would include `.storybook/` files and potentially conflict with Storybook's own build. A dedicated doc tsconfig scoped to `src/**/*.ts` keeps Compodoc's compilation isolated. - **`input()` / `output()` signal API**: Angular 21.x uses signal-based inputs/outputs by default. Compodoc 1.2.1 reads these as properties; JSDoc on the field (not the decorator) is what Compodoc surfaces in the Docs tab. - **Risk -- `documentation.json` not committed**: The generated `documentation.json` must be gitignored or the Storybook build will depend on a generated artifact. The `storybook`/`build-storybook` targets with `compodoc: true` regenerate it on every run, so it should be gitignored. ## Dependencies Steps 1-8 are independent file edits and can be done in any order. Step 9 (install + run) must come last. Within the file edits, steps 5 and 6 depend on step 2 having established where `documentation.json` will be written. ## Verdict **Verdict scope:** scope: api-correctness The plan covers correctness of the Compodoc + Storybook Angular integration and the signal input/output API usage. It does NOT cover security threats (use `/lz-advisor.security-review` after implementation), performance, or accessibility.

Because Nx runs from the workspace root, all paths in `compodocArgs` must be workspace-relative: ```json "compodoc": true, "compodocArgs": [ "-e", "json", "-d", "packages/ngx-smart-components", "-p", "packages/ngx-smart-components/tsconfig.doc.json" ] ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 3 -- `-d` sets where `documentation.json` is written (the project root); `-p` points Compodoc at the correct tsconfig. 4. **Register @storybook/addon-docs in main.ts** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/.storybook/main.ts` - Change: Add `'@storybook/addon-docs'` to the `addons` array. The current array is empty. - Result: ```ts addons: ['@storybook/addon-docs'], ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 4 -- Storybook 10 requires explicit addon registration; without this the Docs tab does not appear. 5. **Wire setCompodocJson in preview.ts** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/.storybook/preview.ts` - Change: Replace the empty file with: ```ts import type { Preview } from '@storybook/angular'; import { setCompodocJson } from '@storybook/addon-docs/angular'; import docJson from '../documentation.json'; setCompodocJson(docJson); const preview: Preview = { parameters: { docs: { source: { type: 'code' }, }, }, }; export default preview; ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 5 -- `setCompodocJson` loads the Compodoc output into Storybook so JSDoc descriptions appear in the Docs tab. 6. **Update .storybook/tsconfig.json to resolve documentation.json** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/.storybook/tsconfig.json` - Change: Add `"resolveJsonModule": true` to `compilerOptions` and add `"../documentation.json"` to the `include` array so TypeScript can import the generated JSON file. - Rationale: Advisor step 6 -- without `resolveJsonModule` the `import docJson from '../documentation.json'` in preview.ts will fail to compile. 7. **Add signal inputs/outputs and JSDoc to the component** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/src/lib/ngx-smart-components/ngx-smart-components.ts` - Change: Import `input` and `output` from `@angular/core`, add JSDoc above the class and above each signal property: ```ts import { ChangeDetectionStrategy, Component, ViewEncapsulation, input, output, } from '@angular/core'; /** * A smart component that demonstrates Compodoc integration with Storybook. */ @Component({ selector: 'smart-ngx-smart-components', imports: [], template: `

{{ label() }}

`, styles: ` :host { display: block; } `, encapsulation: ViewEncapsulation.Emulated, changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush, }) export class NgxSmartComponents { /** The label text displayed inside the component. */ readonly label = input('NgxSmartComponents works!'); /** Emitted when the component is clicked. */ readonly clicked = output(); } ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 7 -- signal-based `input()` and `output()` are the modern Angular API; JSDoc above class and each signal is what Compodoc reads to populate the Docs tab descriptions. 8. **Add autodocs tag to the story** - File: `packages/ngx-smart-components/src/lib/ngx-smart-components/ngx-smart-components.stories.ts` - Change: Add `tags: ['autodocs']` to the `meta` object so Storybook generates the Docs page. Also update the `Primary` story's `args` to exercise the `label` input: ```ts const meta: Meta = { component: NgxSmartComponents, title: 'NgxSmartComponents', tags: ['autodocs'], }; ``` - Rationale: Advisor step 8 -- `tags: ['autodocs']` triggers Storybook's autodocs renderer; Compodoc provides the JSDoc descriptions that populate the auto-generated Docs page. 9. **Install dependencies and verify** - Command: `npm install && npx nx run ngx-smart-components:storybook` - Rationale: Advisor step 9 -- `npm install` pins `@compodoc/compodoc` as a direct dep; `nx run storybook` triggers Compodoc to generate `documentation.json` then serves Storybook with the Docs tab populated. ## Key Decisions - **`@storybook/addon-docs` explicit registration**: Storybook 10 no longer auto-includes addon-docs; it must be listed in `addons`. Skipping this causes a blank/missing Docs tab. - **`-d packages/ngx-smart-components` for `compodocArgs`**: Compodoc writes `documentation.json` to the directory specified by `-d`. The path must be workspace-root-relative because Nx executes the executor from the workspace root. Setting it to the project root means `preview.ts` can import it as `../documentation.json`. - **`tsconfig.doc.json` as a separate tsconfig**: Using the library tsconfig directly would include `.storybook/` files and potentially conflict with Storybook's own build. A dedicated doc tsconfig scoped to `src/**/*.ts` keeps Compodoc's compilation isolated. - **`input()` / `output()` signal API**: Angular 21.x uses signal-based inputs/outputs by default. Compodoc 1.2.1 reads these as properties; JSDoc on the field (not the decorator) is what Compodoc surfaces in the Docs tab. - **Risk -- `documentation.json` not committed**: The generated `documentation.json` must be gitignored or the Storybook build will depend on a generated artifact. The `storybook`/`build-storybook` targets with `compodoc: true` regenerate it on every run, so it should be gitignored. ## Dependencies Steps 1-8 are independent file edits and can be done in any order. Step 9 (install + run) must come last. Within the file edits, steps 5 and 6 depend on step 2 having established where `documentation.json` will be written. ## Verdict **Verdict scope:** scope: api-correctness The plan covers correctness of the Compodoc + Storybook Angular integration and the signal input/output API usage. It does NOT cover security threats (use `/lz-advisor.security-review` after implementation), performance, or accessibility.
